title: Variables and Types
description: Store data in variables and work with basic Python types.
objectives: Understand int, float, str, and bool types

completionRules.expected:
Name: Alice
Age: 25
Height: 1.68 meters
Student: True

recap: Variables store values that you can reuse and update. Python figures out the type (int, float, str, bool) automatically.
practicePrompts: Create a variable for temperature in Celsius and convert it to Fahrenheit.
Try swapping two variables without using a third variable.

practiceExercises.title: Celsius to Fahrenheit
practiceExercises.prompt: Store a temperature in Celsius (use 20), convert it to Fahrenheit using the formula F = C * 9/5 + 32, and print the result in the form `20C = 68.0F`.
practiceExercises.goal: Print the conversion using an f-string.
practiceExercises.starterCode:
celsius = 20
# Convert to Fahrenheit and print "20C = 68.0F"

practiceExercises.title: Swap two values
practiceExercises.prompt: Start with `a = 1` and `b = 2`. Swap the values so that `a` becomes 2 and `b` becomes 1, then print them in the form `a=2, b=1`.
practiceExercises.goal: Swap two variables and print the result.
practiceExercises.starterCode:
a = 1
b = 2
# Swap the values, then print: a=2, b=1
hints: Python lets you swap in one line: `a, b = b, a`.
Or use a temporary variable: tmp = a; a = b; b = tmp.

practiceExercises.title: Profile card
practiceExercises.prompt:
Create variables `name = "Bob"`, `age = 30`, and `is_member = True`. Print them on three separate lines in this format:
Name: Bob
Age: 30
Member: True
practiceExercises.goal: Use three variables with an f-string to format a profile.
practiceExercises.starterCode:
name = "Bob"
age = 30
is_member = True
# Print a three-line profile card
hints: Use three print() calls, one per line.
F-strings can embed any variable: f"Name: {name}".
